g.data-narration: Welcome to Virtual Machines - the backbone of modern cloud computing and data centers.
A Virtual Machine is software that emulates a physical computer, allowing multiple OS to run on one machine.
g.text: A software-based emulation of a physical computer that runs its own OS, as if it were
a separate physical machine. The hypervisor abstracts hardware resources.

g.data-narration: Traditional systems run one OS per hardware, while virtualization creates multiple virtual environments on one machine.
Understanding virtualization requires knowing key terms: Host is the physical machine, Guest is the VM, and the Hypervisor manages them.
g.text: The hypervisor sits between hardware and guest VMs, allocating resources from the host.

g.data-narration: Type 1 hypervisors run directly on hardware without a host OS, providing maximum performance for data centers.
g.text: Runs directly on hardware with no host OS. Maximum performance and security.

g.data-narration: Popular Type 1 hypervisors include VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, Xen, and KVM used in enterprise data centers.
g.text: ✓ High Performance ✓ Better Security ✓ Used in Data Centers ✓ Requires dedicated hardware

g.data-narration: Type 2 hypervisors run as applications on a host OS, easier to set up but with more overhead than Type 1.
g.text: Runs on top of host OS as an application. Easier to set up, good for desktop use.

g.data-narration: Popular Type 2 hypervisors include Oracle VirtualBox, VMware Workstation for development and testing.
g.text: ✓ Easy to install ✓ Good for Development/Testing ✓ Runs on desktop OS ✗ Lower performance

g.data-narration: Compare Type 1 and Type 2 hypervisors: Type 1 runs on bare metal (higher performance), Type 2 runs on host OS (easier setup).
Key benefits include isolation (crash doesn't spread) and server consolidation (saving hardware costs by merging servers).
g.text: Each VM is isolated - crash/virus in one

g.data-narration: Additional benefits include safe testing environments, security through sandboxing, and instant recovery via snapshots.
The Java Virtual Machine is an application-level VM that runs Java bytecode, enabling platform-independent execution.
g.text: Application-level VM - runs Java bytecode for platform independence (Write Once, Run Anywhere)
JVM virtualizes a runtime environment, not hardware. Different from system VMs like VMware.

g.data-narration: Para-virtualization modifies guest OS to use hypercalls instead of trapping privileged instructions, improving performance.
g.text: Guest OS is modified to be aware of virtualization. Uses hypercalls instead of trapping privileged instructions.
Full Virtualization: Privileged instructions TRAPPED by hypervisor
✓ Better performance than full virtualization ✗ Requires modified guest OS Example: Xen

g.data-narration: Modern CPUs provide hardware support like Intel VT-x and AMD-V to reduce virtualization overhead significantly.
g.text: Modern CPUs provide hardware extensions to reduce virtualization overhead significantly.
These technologies allow guest OS code to run directly on CPU in special mode, reducing overhead.

g.data-narration: In summary, VMs use hypervisors to create isolated environments, enabling cloud computing and modern infrastructure.
For exams: Know Type 1 vs Type 2 differences, memorize 5+ benefits, practice drawing hypervisor diagrams.
